coredata.dc:description: Mercury ion (Hg2+) pose a significant hazard to the natural environment. Conventional techniques like Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, X-ray absorption spectroscopy, among others, pose some disadvantages as they demand a lot of money, need trained employees, and cannot provide on-site detection in real-time. A smartphone sensing technique based on silicon quantum dots (Si-QDs) was presented to detect Hg2+ in the environment without the usage of sophisticated equipment. Meanwhile, the technology was built by utilizing a smartphone to capture gray values of fluorescent images of the Si-QDs-Hg2+ system. Microwave-assisted Si-QDs with tiny particle size, high fluorescence, and good optical stability were created. The fluorescence of the Si-QDs was gradually quenched by raising the Hg2+ concentration from 0.5 μmol/L to 5.0 μmol/L for fluorescent detection with a detection limit of 28 nmol/L. The 94.8–97.1 % recovery demonstrated the viability of the Si-QDs approach for detecting Hg2+. Meanwhile, a smartphone sensing strategy was built by recording the gray value of the fluorescent images of the Si-QDs-Hg2+ systems using a smartphone, and the detection limit of the established approach was 3 nmol/L. The accuracy and reliability of the smartphone strategy were verified with the recovery rates of 80.3–92.5 % in tap water and 87.6–109 % in river water. Electron transfer quenching mechanism between Si-QDs and Hg2+ was evidenced by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, fluorescent decay curves, cyclic voltammetry, and Zeta potential. Finally, the suggested approach was used to detect Hg2+ in water samples from various environments.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent sensing technology based on Si-QDs incorporated with a smartphone facilitates swift and on-site Hg2+ detection in ambient water.

Mercury ion (Hg2+) pose a significant hazard to the natural environment. Conventional techniques like Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, X-ray absorption spectroscopy, among others, pose some disadvantages as they demand a lot of money, need trained employees, and cannot provide on-site detection in real-time. A smartphone sensing technique based on silicon quantum dots (Si-QDs) was presented to detect Hg2+ in the environment without the usage of sophisticated equipment. Meanwhile, the technology was built by utilizing a smartphone to capture gray values of fluorescent images of the Si-QDs-Hg2+ system. Microwave-assisted Si-QDs with tiny particle size, high fluorescence, and good optical stability were created. The fluorescence of the Si-QDs was gradually quenched by raising the Hg2+ concentration from 0.5 μmol/L to 5.0 μmol/L for fluorescent detection with a detection limit of 28 nmol/L. The 94.8–97.1 % recovery demonstrated the viability of the Si-QDs approach for detecting Hg2+. Meanwhile, a smartphone sensing strategy was built by recording the gray value of the fluorescent images of the Si-QDs-Hg2+ systems using a smartphone, and the detection limit of the established approach was 3 nmol/L. The accuracy and reliability of the smartphone strategy were verified with the recovery rates of 80.3–92.5 % in tap water and 87.6–109 % in river water. Electron transfer quenching mechanism between Si-QDs and Hg2+ was evidenced by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, fluorescent decay curves, cyclic voltammetry, and Zeta potential. Finally, the suggested approach was used to detect Hg2+ in water samples from various environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions have created major environmental and human health concerns due to their high biological toxicity, and accumulation along the food chain [1–4]. Mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most hazardous heavy metal ions for its persistence, low density, high toxicity, and carcinogenicity [5–7]. The US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) states that the permissible limit for Hg2+ in drinking water is 10 nmol/L and The PRC State Environmental Protection Administration (SEPA) stipulates that the mercury limitation of surface water in the environment is 5 nmol/L. It is critical to create simple, sensitive, and accurate technologies to detect Hg2+ to protect the safety of the environment and humans. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [8,9], X-ray absorption spectroscopy [10,11], surface enhanced Raman scattering [12,13], and cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry [14] have all been used to detect Hg2+. However, they necessitated the complex operation, relied on laboratory and expert technicians, and could not match the on-site and real-time detection requirements. The fluorescent method has gained popularity owing to the advantages including low cost, ease of operation, rapid response, high sensitivity and good selectivity [15,16], basing on various fluorescent probes including organic fluorescent dyes [17–19], noble metal clusters [20–24], carbon dots [25], quantum dots (QDs) [26], etc. Nevertheless, the synthesis of organic fluorescent dyes is intricate and they frequently suffer from poor photostability. QDs contain toxic components (e.g. Cd and Pb elements), while noble metal clusters are expensive to prepare.

Silicon quantum dots (Si-QDs) have gained popularity as an alternative to classical II-VI QDs due to their ease of synthesis, low cost, great optical characteristics, resistance to photobleaching, low biotoxicity, and environmental friendliness [27–30]. The fluorescent sensing applications of Si-QDs for detection of hydrogen sulfide [31], VB12 [32], Cu2+ [15], nitrite [33], hydroquinone [34], and other compounds have made tremendous development. Copper functionalized silicon quantum dots (Cu-Si-QDs) were effectively generated by Zhou et al. [35] for the detection of bisulfite in atmospheric particulates. Ma et al. [36] suggested using Si-QDs and copper nanoclusters to detect L-cysteine in milk by radiometric fluorescent sensing. Liu et al. [37] created F-doped silicon quantum dots (F-Si-QDs) with high dispersibility and optical stability for detecting new coccine in food samples. However, on-site, highly efficient detection was still not possible if only quantum dots are used for detection. Nowadays, smartphone-based sensing technology has been built to satisfy on-site and convenient testing of a variety of targets, including numerous heavy metal ions [38], tetracycline [39], organophosphorus pesticides [40], glutathione and azodicarbonamide [41]. Not only has it achieved timely detection in the field, but also efficient detection of multiple samples simultaneously. To the best of our knowledge, no smartphone-based portable device is equipped with Si-QDs for Hg2+ detection.

Herein, we developed a new smartphone-based sensing technique that utilized Si-QDs as the fluorescent probe to detect Hg2+ in the ambient waters on-site and in real-time. N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylene diamine (DAMO) and trisodium citrate dehydrate were employed as the silicon source and reducing agent to produce Si-QDs by microwave-assisted method. The fluorescent emission of Si-QDs was quenched in the presence of Hg2+, and the fluorescent quenching pictures could be recorded by a smartphone-based simple and visible sensing strategy to recognize the mono-fluorescence from Si-QDs for the detection of Hg2+. The fluorescent quenching process was investigated by UV–vis absorption, cyclic voltammetry, fluorescent decay curve and Zeta potential. And the energy transfer mechanism was proposed for the response of Si-QDs toward Hg2+ in combination with the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). The established detection method was successfully applied for the detection of Hg2+ in various water samples. Hence, we provided a new possibility for the portable real-time and on-site detection of Hg2+ with the combination of Si-QDs and smartphone.

The fluorescence Si-QDs were prepared according to a previous method [42]. Firstly, Ar-saturated glycerol solution was prepared in a 100 mL flask, and then 0.3180 g trisodium citrate dehydrate was added into the flask. After stirring vigorously, 3 mL DAMO was subsequently dropped into the homogeneous reaction system with continuous stirring for several minutes. Finally, the as-prepared precursor solution was moved into the atmospheric microwave reactor at 180 °C for 15 min to synthesize Si-QDs as confirmed with the color change to dark brown. The crude solution was purified by dialysis with the molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da for 24 h to yield the purty Si-QDs.

50 µL of 18.7 mg/L fresh Si-QDs solution and different volumes of 10 mg/mL Hg2+ solutions were added into the centrifugal tubes with the final volume of 1.5 mL, which were fixed by PBS buffer solution (pH 7.2). The mixtures were stirred and incubated for 15 min, and the fluorescent detection was performed with the excitation wavelength of 367 nm under a 1 cm-width fluorescent cuvette. Each experiment was repeated in three independent parallels to ensure the accuracy of test results. And the quenching dynamics was described with the Stern-Volmer equation:
(1)
F 0
F
=
K SV
Q
+ 1
where F0 and F refer to the fluorescent intensities of Si-QDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. Ksv represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the Hg2+ concentration.

An ultraviolet obscura and android smartphone equipped with a color recognition software was utilized to fabricate a smartphone sensing platform for visual detection of Hg2+ on site and in real time. A series of distinctive concentrations of Hg2+ solutions were placed into the ultraviolet obscura and excited under a 365 nm UV lamp. The fluorescent images were captured by the android smartphone and the RGB values were recorded by the color recognition software, which were converted into the gray value according to the formula [43]:
(2)
Grayvalue = R × 0.299 + G × 0.587 + B × 0.144
where R, G and B represent red, green, and blue, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental water samples were adopted as the real samples to evaluate the practicability of the proposed method, including the tap water, surface water, seawater, and river water samples. The water samples were centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min to remove particulate impurities. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected for the detection of Hg2+. A stock of Hg2+ solution was spiked in the tap water, which was performed three times for each sample under the same operation.

The composition of the Si-QDs was confirmed further by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements, which evidenced that the surface of Si-QDs was mainly composed of C, N, O, and Si elements (Fig. 2 A). In C 1 s spectra of Si-QDs, the peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, and 287.8 eV were attributed to C–C/C-Si, C-O, and C=O bonds [46,47], respectively (Fig. 2B). The N 1 s spectra were constituted by the peaks of 398.9 eV and 399.8 eV for the presence of N-C and N–H bonds (Fig. 2C) [29]. In O 1 s spectra, the peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.0 eV and 533.5 eV were ascribed to C=O, C-O and Si-O bonds [48], separately (Fig. 2D). The peak at 101.4 eV in Si 2p spectra (Fig. 2E) was assigned to Si-C bond and the one at 101.9 eV to Si-O bond [31]. The results of XPS measurements were agreement with that of FTIR spectrum and further indicated the preparation of Si-QDs.

As shown in Fig. 2F, Si-QDs displayed a wide absorption peak around 356 nm in the UV–vis spectrum (black line in Fig. 2F). Correspondingly, Si-QDs showed an obvious excitation peak of 367 nm (red line in Fig. 2F) and a symmetrical emission peak was observed at 464 nm (blue line in Fig. 2F). Moreover, the Si-QDs solution was transparent and emitted blue fluorescence under daylight and 365 nm, respectively (inset of Fig. 2F).

The emission wavelength of the Si-QDs remained nearly constant with varied excitation from 310 to 390 nm at equal intervals (Fig. S2A), demonstrating the excitation-independent emission feature of the as-resulted Si-QDs, which was consistent with the prior reported [49–51]. As observed in Fig. S2B, the Si-QDs also emitted the fluorescence at 464 nm under the excitation from 600 nm to 800 nm and demonstrated the excellent up-conversion fluorescence from the two-photon absorption mechanism [52]. According to Eq. S (1) [53,54], the relative photoluminescence quantum yield of Si-QDs was 29.4 % (Fig. S2C). The fluorescent intensity of the Si-QDs remained essentially unaltered under 367 nm stimulation for 3 h and even 25 days held at room temperature, as shown in Fig. S2D and S2E. Meanwhile, the fluorescent intensity of the as-prepared Si-QDs had a negligible effect with the salt concentration increasing from 0 to 2 mol/L (Fig. S3). As a result, the Si-QDs exhibited the great optical stability, low photobleaching and strong salt resistance.

The experimental parameters including detection duration and pH value were tuned to produce outstanding sensing performance for Hg2+ detection. The fluorescent intensities of Si-QDs showed a modest decrease with detection periods ranging from 0 to 15 min, and achieved an equilibrium condition with the long detection time, as shown in Fig. S4A, hence 15 min was chosen as the ideal detection time for Hg2+ detection. The fluorescent intensity of Si-QDs did not vary considerably in different pH values of PBS buffers, as shown in Fig. S4B, demonstrating the good pH stability of the Si-QDs-Hg2+ system. To deal with the complex environment, PBS buffer (10 mmol/L, pH 7.2) was used for Hg2+ detection.

Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescent of Si-QDs decreased gradually along with the concentrations of Hg2+ from 0 to 16 μmol/L (Fig. 3 A). Fig. 3B showed the concentration-dependent fluorescent intensity of Si-QDs towards Hg2+. The relationship between F0/F-1 and Hg2+ concentration from 0.5 to 5.0 μmol/L showed a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9989) and a limit of detection (LOD) of the method was 28 nmol/L (LOD=3 σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and s is the slope of the corresponding calibration curve) (Fig. 3C). The inset photo in Fig. 3B demonstrated that the blue fluorescence weakens slowly as the Hg2+ concentration increased, which intuitively evidenced the ability of Hg2+ to quench the fluorescence of Si-QDs. The CIE chromaticity diagrams of different Hg2+ concentrations were plotted with CIE1931 software. As displayed in Fig. 3D, the fluorescent color turned from blue to light blue with the increased Hg2+ concentration, which was consistent with the imaging results in Fig. 3B. The results verified the feasibility of determining Hg2+ using Si-QDs as a probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Hg2+ via smartphone strategy
ce:section.ce:para: A simple technique based on the combination of a portable UV equipment and a smartphone equipped with color recognition software was developed to provide on-site, real-time, and rapid detection of Hg2+ in environmental samples. Fig. 4 A and S5 showed the detection schematic diagram of smartphone sensing platform and the process of RGB value recognition. Table S1 listed specific RGB values obtained by the smartphone APP. As shown in Fig. 4B, the gray values gradually decreased as Hg2+ concentration increased from 0 to 30 μmol/L, and the blue fluorescence of Si-QDs under 365 nm irradiation slowly darkened with the enlarging Hg2+ concentration (the upper illustration in Fig. 4B). The lower inset of Fig. 4B exhibited a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9998) between the gray values and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0–5 μmol/L. LOD of the established smartphone-based sensing method for Hg2+ was 3 nmol/L (S/N=3), which was less than the limit value specified by EPA (10 nmol/L) and PRC SEPA (5 nmol/L), indicating that the feasibility of using smartphone-based device to on-site and real-time detection Hg2+ in environmental waters. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the photo of “D”, “L”, “O”, “U”, “&”, “E”, “C”, “S”, and “F” refer to the Si-QDs cultured with 0, 1, 2, 3, 3.5, 4, 4.5, 10, 30 μmol/L Hg2+ solutions, respectively. The brightness of the letters gradually decreased with the increase of Hg2+ concentration. Table S2 detailed the performance of fluorescent probes and the smartphone approach for detecting mercury ion [55–73]. The results achieved with our approach were equivalent to those previously described, and the fabrication of Si-QDs was significantly simpler and less expensive, while the smartphone strategy offered visual in situ detection of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent quenching mechanism of Si-QDs by Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between Hg2+ and the surface hydroxyl and/or amino groups of Si-QDs caused the fluorescent quenching [74], which was investigated with UV absorption spectra, Zeta potential, and fluorescent decay curve. The UV absorption peak of Si-QDs was almost unchanged as the Hg2+ concentration increased from 0 to 5 μmol/L, which suggested the presence of dynamic quenching effect (Fig. 5 A). As described in Fig. S6, the UV absorption spectrum of Hg2+ (red line) slightly overlaps with that of Si-QDs (black line) and the excitation spectrum (light blue line) of Si-QDs, which implied that the fluorescent quenching was mainly derived from electron transfer (ET) and internal filtration effect (IFE) [75–77]. Meanwhile, there was nearly no overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of Hg2+ (red line) and the emission spectrum of Si-QDs (dark blue line), which indicated that no the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurred [78]. The fluorescent decay curves further revealed the interaction between Si-QDs and Hg2+(Fig. 5B). Furthermore, the fluorescent lifetimes of Si-QDs gradually decreased with the addition of Hg2+ from 0 to 3.0 μmol/L, which also indicated the contribution of the dynamic quenching effect to the detection of Hg2+ with Si-QDs as probe. The interaction between Si-QDs and Hg2+ was confirmed by the Zeta potential of Si-QDs changing from −2.01 mV to 6.14 mV following the addition of 1 μmol/L Hg2+ (Fig. 5C).

To verify the ET quenching mechanism, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of Si-QDs were researched by cyclic voltammetry. As shown in Fig. S7A, the apparent reduction potential of Si-QDs was tested as −0.88 eV, the same to the previous result [79]. The ELUMO and EHOMO of Si-QDs were counted with the following empirical formulas [80]:
(3)
E LUMO
=- e
E red
+ 4.4
(4)
E HOMO
=
E LUMO
-
E g
Ered and Eg refer to the reduction potential and band gap of Si-QDs, respectively. Thus, the ELUMO of Si-QDs was −3.52 eV by Eq (3). Fig. S7B showed the Eg of Si-QDs drawn by its UV absorption spectrum, and Eg was calculated as 3.02 eV. Thus, the EHOMO of Si-QDs was calculated as −6.54 eV by Eq (4). Fig. 5D showed the schematic diagram of energy levels for Si-QDs. The electrode potential of Hg2+ was −5.25 eV [25,50], which was higher than the HOMO of Si-QDs and lower than LUMO of Si-QDs. Therefore, the electron could transfer from the LUMO of Si-QDs to Hg2+, to cause the fluorescent quenching of Si-QDs.

To study the selectivity and anti-interference for Hg2+ using Si-QDs as a probe, a variety of CH3Hg+, As3+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Mn2+, Na+, Li+, K+, Cd2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Cs+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Sr2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, F−, CH3COO−, S2O3 2−, CO3 2−, I−, CN−, B4O7 2− and Hg2+ solutions were used. As shown in Fig. 6 A and 6B, the fluorescent intensity of Si-QDs was drastically reduced with the addition of Hg2+, but essentially unaltered by the addition of the other ions, demonstrating that the Si-QDs had excellent ions selectivity for Hg2+ detection. The inset in Fig. 6A displayed obviously the fluorescent brightness of Si-QDs darken visibly in the presence of Hg2+ in comparison with other ions, which also implied the satisfactory selectivity of the developed method using Si-QDs as the fluorescent probe for Hg2+ detection. Furthermore, in the presence of Hg2+, the fluorescent intensities of Si-QDs were less affected by the addition of the other ions, indicating the strong anti-interference capacity (Fig. 6C and 6D) of the proposed detection method for Hg2+ based on the combination of Si-QDs and smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The developed detection approach for Hg2+ was further evaluated the accuracy, reproducibility, and practicality in actual samples by the spiked tests. In many national standards, it was considered feasible with the recovery between 70 % to 120 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) of less than 20 %. As listed in Tables 1 and 2
, under the different spiked levels of Hg2+ ion, the recoveries of the fluorescent approach were in the range 94.8–97.1 % in tap water, 95.9–97.4 % in surface water, 91.5–95.5 % in river water and 106–113 % in seawater. The RSD of fluorescent approach were less than 4.67 %. Moreover, the recoveries of the smartphone-based strategy varied from 80.3 to 92.5 % in tap water (Table 1) and from 87.6 to 109 % in river water (Fig. S8 and Table S3) with the low RSD less than 18.8 % under the different spiked levels of Hg2+ ion, which was agreement with the results of fluorescent approach, suggesting that the accuracy and feasibleness of the established methods to detect Hg2+ in real samples. Subsequently, the proposed method was applied to detect Hg2+ in different water samples. And, no Hg2+ was found in the collected surface water, seawater, and river water samples, which was consistent with the reported [81,82], manifesting the safety of the environment waters for human health.

In summary, we proposed a facile and feasible method to detect Hg2+ via smartphone-based sensing strategy combining with the novel Si-QDs. The synthesis of Si-QDs was simple, low cost, short time and eco-friendly. The fluorescence of Si-QDs was quenched by Hg2+, as validated with the results of UV–vis, fluorescent decay, Zeta potential, cyclic voltammetry, HOMO-LUMO. The electron transfer mechanism was proved between Si-QDs and Hg2+. In the fluorescent detection, the fluorescent quenching of Si-QDs was linearly dependent on the Hg2+ concentration ranging from 0.5 to 5.0 μmol/L with the LOD of 28 nmol/L and the spiked recoveries of 94.8 % to 97.1 %. The linearity in the smartphone-based method spanned from 0 to 5 μmol/L, with a LOD of 3 nmol/L and spiking recoveries ranging from 80.3 % to 109 %. Meanwhile, the suggested approach demonstrated excellent selectivity and anti-interference capability. As a result, we presented the smartphone sensing platform as a simple, rapid, and efficient technique for detecting Hg2+ in environmental water in real time and on-site.
